title: Package Management
description: Package management is how Linux systems install, update, and remove software. At Enterprise, you'll work across Debian-based (Ubuntu), RHEL-based (Amazon Linux 2/2023), and containerized environments. Understanding the differences between dpkg/APT, rpm/YUM/DNF, and compiling from source is essential for building reproducible infrastructure.

sections.title: Debian/Ubuntu: APT & dpkg
sections.description: Ubuntu (the most common dev environment at Enterprise) uses APT (Advanced Package Tool) on top of dpkg. Packages are .deb files. /etc/apt/sources.list and /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ define repositories. Always run apt update before apt install — this refreshes the package index.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Repository management ===
cat /etc/apt/sources.list                    # Main repos
ls /etc/apt/sources.list.d/                  # Third-party repos (Docker, Kubernetes, etc.)

# === APT commands ===
sudo apt update                               # Refresh package index (must run first!)
sudo apt upgrade                              # Upgrade all packages
sudo apt full-upgrade                         # Upgrade with dependency changes
sudo apt autoremove                           # Remove orphaned dependencies

sudo apt install nginx                        # Install package
sudo apt install -y nginx                     # Non-interactive (script-friendly)
sudo apt remove nginx                         # Remove package (leave config)
sudo apt purge nginx                          # Remove package + config files

apt list --installed                          # List installed packages
apt list --upgradable                         # List available updates
apt search "web server"                       # Search packages
apt show nginx                                # Show package details

# === dpkg (low-level) ===
dpkg -i package.deb                           # Install .deb file directly
dpkg -r package                              # Remove package
dpkg -l                                       # List installed packages
dpkg -L nginx                                 # List files owned by package
dpkg -S /usr/bin/nvim                         # Find which package owns a file

# === Fix broken dependencies ===
sudo apt --fix-broken install                 # Repair broken dpkg state

# === Pinning package versions ===
# /etc/apt/preferences.d/pin-nginx
# Package: nginx
# Pin: version 1.24.*
# Pin-Priority: 1001

# === Installing specific version ===
apt list -a nginx                             # All available versions
sudo apt install nginx=1.24.0-1ubuntu1        # Install specific version
sudo apt-mark hold nginx                      # Prevent automatic upgrade
sudo apt-mark unhold nginx                    # Re-enable upgrades

sections.title: RHEL/CentOS/Amazon Linux: RPM & YUM/DNF
sections.description: Amazon Linux 2 and Amazon Linux 2023 (the standard at AWS/Enterprise) are RHEL-based. They use RPM (.rpm packages) with YUM (Amazon Linux 2) or DNF (Amazon Linux 2023) as the high-level package manager. The underlying tool is rpm.
sections.codeBlock:
# === YUM (Amazon Linux 2, CentOS 7) ===
sudo yum update -y                             # Update all packages
sudo yum install -y nginx                      # Install
sudo yum remove nginx                          # Remove
sudo yum list installed                        # List installed
yum list available "nginx*"                    # Search available
yum info nginx                                 # Package details
sudo yum autoremove                            # Clean orphaned deps
sudo yum history                               # Transaction history

# === DNF (Amazon Linux 2023, Fedora, RHEL 9) ===
sudo dnf install -y nginx
sudo dnf remove nginx
sudo dnf upgrade --refresh
sudo dnf list --installed
sudo dnf repolist                               # List configured repos
sudo dnf provides /usr/bin/nginx                # Which package provides a file?
sudo dnf history

# === RPM (low-level) ===
rpm -ivh package.rpm                           # Install RPM
rpm -e package                                 # Remove RPM
rpm -qa                                        # Query all installed packages
rpm -ql nginx                                  # List files owned by package
rpm -qf /usr/sbin/nginx                        # Find package owning a file
rpm -qi nginx                                  # Package info

# === EPEL (Extra Packages for Enterprise Linux) ===
sudo amazon-linux-extras install epel          # Amazon Linux 2
sudo dnf install epel-release                  # Amazon Linux 2023 / RHEL 9

# === Amazon Linux Extras ===
sudo amazon-linux-extras list                  # Available topics
sudo amazon-linux-extras install nginx1        # Install from extras

# === Locking versions ===
echo "exclude=nginx*" | sudo tee -a /etc/yum.conf  # Prevent updates (YUM)
sudo dnf versionlock add nginx*                # Lock version (DNF)

sections.title: Compiling from Source & Alternatives
sections.description: Sometimes you need software not in package repos, or you need specific compile-time flags. At Enterprise, critical services are often compiled with custom optimization flags (PGO, LTO, specific CPU architectures). Understanding the build chain (configure, make, make install) is essential for infrastructure engineers.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Classic build chain ===
wget https://example.com/software-1.0.tar.gz
tar -xzf software-1.0.tar.gz
cd software-1.0/

# Step 1: Configure (check dependencies, set options)
./configure --prefix=/usr/local --enable-optimizations

# Step 2: Build
make -j$(nproc)                                # Parallel build using all cores

# Step 3: Install
sudo make install                              # Copy to system directories

# === Common configure flags ===
# --prefix=/usr          — Install to /usr (not /usr/local)
# --sysconfdir=/etc      — Config files location
# --with-ssl             — Enable SSL support
# --enable-debug         — Build with debug symbols

# === Checkinstall (create a .deb/.rpm from source) ===
sudo apt install checkinstall
sudo checkinstall make install                 # Creates and installs a .deb

# === Stow (manage manually compiled software) ===
# Install each version under /usr/local/stow/<pkg>-<ver>/
# Symlink to /usr/local/ with stow
sudo stow -d /usr/local/stow -S nginx-1.24

# === Enterprise pattern: hermetic builds ===
# At Enterprise, we don't compile on production servers.
# Builds happen in hermetic build systems (e.g., Bazel, Buck).
# Artifacts are versioned and deployed via package managers or containers.

# === Alternatives: Homebrew on Linux ===
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
brew install nginx
brew services start nginx

sections.title: Docker & Containers: The Modern "Package"
sections.description: At Enterprise, most services are packaged as container images rather than native packages. Containers bundle the application + its dependencies + the package manager snapshot. Understanding the Dockerfile and multi-stage builds is essential for modern infrastructure.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Dockerfile with package management ===
FROM ubuntu:22.04 AS builder

# Always combine apt update + install in ONE RUN layer
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    build-essential \
    libssl-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Multi-stage build: builder → runtime
FROM ubuntu:22.04 AS runtime

# Copy only the compiled binary, not the build tools
COPY --from=builder /app/compiled_binary /usr/local/bin/

# Distroless images (no package manager at all)
FROM gcr.io/distroless/base-debian12:latest

# === Best practices ===
# 1. Pin base image digests (not tags)
#    FROM ubuntu:22.04@sha256:abc123...
# 2. Combine RUN apt-get update && apt-get install (cache busting)
# 3. Clean apt cache in same layer: rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
# 4. Use --no-install-recommends to reduce image size
# 5. Never leave a package manager in the final image

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'You need to install a specific version of nginx on Ubuntu, but apt installs the wrong version. What do you do?'

A: 'First, run apt list -a nginx to see all available versions. If my version is there, install it with apt install nginx=<version>. If not, I'd add the official nginx repository to /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ with the correct distribution codename. As a last resort, I'd download the .deb directly or compile from source. At Enterprise, we would never apt install on production — instead, we'd bake the correct version into an AMI or container image.'

Q: 'What's the difference between apt update and apt upgrade?'

A: 'apt update downloads the latest package lists from repositories — it tells you what's available. apt upgrade actually installs newer versions of installed packages based on those updated lists. Running upgrade without update will give you stale information.'

Q: 'How do package managers resolve dependencies?'

A: 'APT, YUM, and DNF maintain a dependency graph. Each package declares its dependencies (Depends:), conflicts (Conflicts:), and provides (Provides:). The solver finds a consistent set of package versions satisfying all constraints. If there's an unresolvable conflict, the tool reports it. At Enterprise, we avoid these issues by using deterministic builds and container images with fixed package snapshots.'

  • DNF Documentation — Official DNF reference
  • Dockerfile Best Practices — Official Docker guide

analogy.title: Package Manager as a Curated App Store
analogy.description: Repositories are verified warehouses. Dependencies are "this dish requires these ingredients." The package manager resolves the full shopping list automatically. apt/dnf are different store chains - same concept, different inventory.

commonMistakes.solution: Always use `rm -i` or `ls` first to confirm what will be deleted

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Debian/Ubuntu: APT & dpkg
practiceLabs.task: Run `cat` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man cat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: RHEL/CentOS/Amazon Linux: RPM & YUM/DNF
practiceLabs.task: Run `ls` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ls` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Compiling from Source & Alternatives
practiceLabs.task: Run `sudo` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man sudo` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `cat` | Debian/Ubuntu: APT & dpkg |
| `ls` | Debian/Ubuntu: APT & dpkg |
| `sudo` | Debian/Ubuntu: APT & dpkg |
| `sudo` | RHEL/CentOS/Amazon Linux: RPM & YUM/DNF |
| `yum` | RHEL/CentOS/Amazon Linux: RPM & YUM/DNF |
| `rpm` | RHEL/CentOS/Amazon Linux: RPM & YUM/DNF |
| `wget` | Compiling from Source & Alternatives |
| `tar` | Compiling from Source & Alternatives |
| `cd` | Compiling from Source & Alternatives |
| `FROM` | Docker & Containers: The Modern "Package" |
| `RUN` | Docker & Containers: The Modern "Package" |
| `build-essential` | Docker & Containers: The Modern "Package" |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man cat`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: package mgmt operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.symptom: Permission issues accessing package mgmt resources
troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Debian/Ubuntu: APT & dpkg?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Debian/Ubuntu: APT & dpkg is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `cat` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the package mgmt directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a package mgmt directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a package mgmt scenario, your monitoring alerts show cat failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  In 15+ years across multiple FAANG companies, I've seen package management evolve from 'YUM on a fleet of pets' to 'everything runs in containers with immutable base images
FAANG, package updates are applied by rebuilding AMIs or rolling out new container versions — never in-place on running instances
productionTips: Production Tip:  Never run unattended-upgrades on production servers
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, package management is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
